text.title: tracker-github-copilot-workspace Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for tracker-github-copilot-workspace. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: The agent MUST produce four artefacts in strict order: (a) `spec.md` containing current-state and desired-state bullet lists, (b) `plan.md` listing per-file changes with line-range hints, (c) `diff.patch` containing the actual edit, (d) the PR. Each gate emits its artefact, waits for an explicit human "approve" event (UI button click in Workspace, or a `:white_check_mark:` reaction on the gate comment for headless runs), and only then advances. The agent MUST refuse to generate gate N+1 if gate N has no approval event in its journal; refusal lands as a comment "blocked at gate N" and the run aborts.
rule.rationale: GitHub's four-stage gate is the documented Copilot Workspace contract precisely so reviewers can edit the spec/plan cheaply before the diff exists. The ~30% agentic PR fail-rate reported in April 2026 concentrates in runs that skipped intermediate gates, because misread intent flows downstream and burns a review cycle on rework.

rule.statement: Every agent-opened PR MUST include in its body (a) a single line matching `^(Closes|Fixes) #[0-9]+` referencing the source issue, and (b) a Workspace snapshot URL on the line `Workspace snapshot: https://github.com/copilot/spaces/<id>`. A repository CI check MUST grep both lines and fail the PR if either is missing. Editing the PR body to remove either line MUST re-trigger the failing check, blocking merge until restored. The agent MUST NOT edit the snapshot URL after open; if the spec or plan changes, it opens a fresh PR.
rule.rationale: April 2026 saw a documented GitHub bug where Copilot agent stripped the link text from the PR body; the closing keyword's absence left issues open across an entire org. Making both lines required and CI-enforced is the only way to guarantee the round-trip given that any tooling can mutate the body. Snapshot URL stability matters because reviewers cite it in their review comments — overwriting it invalidates that audit trail.
